routing_rule._doc: v2 router. Two changes vs v1 (hybrid-grep-4k-rtk-err-cat-v1): (1) budget raised from 4k to 120k tokens; (2) fallback switched from rtk-err-cat to tail-200. **The 120k threshold was tuned on the same 13-case evaluation data it is then scored against** (see anti_leakage._doc below); this config is an EVALUATION-TUNED PROTOTYPE, not a fully blind generalization result. The prototype demonstrates a hypothesis (a properly-budgeted grep+tail router beats hybrid-v1 on v2) but does not yet PROVE the hypothesis on a hold-out corpus. The corresponding §3e in reports/e10_v2_generalization_partial.md is framed accordingly: hybrid-v2 wins on the calibration data; v3 train/holdout split is required before promoting the result.

anti_leakage._doc: **`uses_diagnosis_eval` is TRUE** for this v2 router and that is a real leakage. The 120k threshold was selected by reading `results/v2/stress/eval_diagnosis_real-debugger-v[12].json` to find the empirical Sonnet/Haiku abstain cliff (between 100k and 161k tokens at the 13-case state — airflow 100k tokens scored cleanly, rust 161k abstained). Those eval files are produced by `tools/evaluate_diagnosis.py`, which loads each case's `ground_truth.json` to compute scores — so the threshold is downstream of ground-truth-aware artifacts even though the router code itself never opens ground_truth.json at runtime. The original Codex review (2026-05-08) flagged the prior version of this comment as a contradiction; this revision is the honest disclosure. The router itself remains `uses_ground_truth: false` and `uses_review_labels: false` — those properties hold for the router code's runtime behavior. The `uses_diagnosis_eval: true` declaration captures the threshold-calibration leakage at the design layer. Hybrid-v1 (4k budget) had the same calibration risk on v1.3 (see cilogbench_v1_3_limitations.md §9); this is being explicit about it for v2.
